passage: 趙州和尚、因僧問、狗子還有佛性也無。州云、無。
notes: A monk asked Zhaozhou: does a dog have Buddha-nature? Zhaozhou said: 無. Wumen's commentary instructs the student to make their whole body a single mass of inquiry concentrated on this character. The koan works not by providing an answer but by becoming the question — 無 as a concentration object that cuts off conceptual elaboration. The monk's question expects a philosophical answer (yes or no, with reasons); Zhaozhou's 無 refuses the frame entirely.

passage: 参禅は須らく祖師の関を透すべし、妙悟は要に心路を絶すべし。
notes: Wumen's verse commentary on Case 1. Zen practice requires passing through the barrier of the patriarchs; subtle awakening requires cutting off the road of the heart-mind. 心路 (the road of the heart-mind) is what 無 is meant to block — the habitual movement of conceptual mind along its familiar paths. Note 心 (kokoro) embedded in 心路.

passage: 色即是空、空即是色。
notes: Form is precisely emptiness, emptiness precisely form. The copula 即是 (precisely is, is identical with) is doing the load-bearing work — this is not a claim that form doesn't exist but that form and emptiness are not two separate things that can be related. They are identical in nature. The passage is the doctrinal center of the Prajnaparamita corpus and is recited daily in Zen monasteries throughout East Asia.

passage: 舎利子、是諸法空相、不生不滅、不垢不浄、不増不減。
notes: Shariputra — the nature of all dharmas is emptiness: not arising, not ceasing; not defiled, not pure; not increasing, not decreasing. The negations (不) cascade through all the categories by which phenomena are ordinarily grasped and sorted. 空相 — the mark or characteristic of emptiness — is what all dharmas share. This passage clarifies that 空 is not a description of a special state but of the nature of all phenomena at all times.

passage: 菩提自性、本来清浄、但用此心、直了成佛。
notes: Bodhi-nature is originally pure; simply use this heart-mind directly and you will attain Buddhahood. The directness of 此心 — this heart-mind, the one already functioning — is the hinge of the Southern school's teaching. Not a heart-mind to be acquired or cultivated into existence, but one already present. Huineng's entire teaching can be read as an unpacking of 此心 — what it means to use directly what is already fully here.

passage: 自性迷即是衆生、自性覚即是佛。
notes: When self-nature (自性) is deluded it is sentient beings; when self-nature is awakened it is Buddha. 心 is implicit throughout — self-nature is the nature of the heart-mind, and its delusion or awakening is what distinguishes ordinary beings from Buddhas. The teaching: the distinction is not ontological (a different kind of being) but functional (the same heart-mind operating under delusion or in clarity).

passage: 趙州、因僧問、如何是道。州云、牆外底。
notes: A monk asks Zhaozhou: what is the Way? Zhaozhou says: the one outside the wall. The answer refuses the philosophical register the question opens — the monk expects 道 to be answered with abstraction, and Zhaozhou points to the concrete particular right there, outside the wall. The koan works because 道 in the monk's question is already loaded with Daoist and Buddhist philosophical weight, and Zhaozhou drops all of it for the ordinary thing at hand.

passage: 南泉云、平常心是道。
notes: Nanquan (Nansen) says: ordinary mind is the Way. 平常心 — ordinary, everyday, unexceptional mind — is 道. This formula is one of the most important in Chan/Zen: the Way is not achieved by extraordinary practice or special states but is the ordinary functioning of the heart-mind right now. The formula requires understanding 心 and 道 simultaneously — ordinary heart-mind is itself the Way, not a sign pointing toward it.

passage: 西洋ニテハ希臘ノ昔ヨリ哲学ト云フ学アリ
notes: In the West, since ancient Greece, there has existed a learning called 哲学. Nishi's framing introduces the coinage in the context of explaining Western intellectual traditions — one discipline among several that Japan needed to acquire and understand. The matter-of-fact tone (there exists a learning called this) is the Meiji translation posture: naming the thing gives Japan a conceptual handle on it.

passage: 社会者、有機体也
notes: Society is an organism. Liang Qichao, writing in Chinese after years in Japan, uses 社会 as a naturalized Chinese term to introduce Herbert Spencer's social organism metaphor — a key concept of nineteenth-century sociology. This moment exemplifies the reverse flow: a Japanese coinage (社会) enters Chinese intellectual discourse and immediately begins doing new theoretical work. Liang does not flag 社会 as a Japanese loan — by 1902 it has become simply the word.

passage: 天は人の上に人を造らず人の下に人を造らずと言えり
notes: Heaven did not create people above other people or below other people. Fukuzawa's opening line — the most widely quoted sentence in Meiji literature. Does not use 民主 directly but establishes the conceptual ground (human equality as natural fact) into which democratic political vocabulary would be planted. The sentence's power comes from its simplicity and its directness against the Confucian hierarchy it implicitly refuses.

passage: 祖以袈裟遮圍、不令人見、為説金剛経。至應無所住而生其心、惠能言下大悟、一切万法、不離自性。
notes: The Fifth Patriarch Hongren conceals Huineng behind a robe and explains the Diamond Sutra. When the recitation reaches the line 'produce a mind that clings to nothing' (應無所住而生其心), Huineng is struck into great awakening (言下大悟): all ten thousand dharmas do not depart from self-nature. Three things deserve attention here. First, the awakening is triggered by a specific moment in a specific text — 悟 is not a product of accumulated effort but an event with a precise occasion. Second, the content of the awakening is not described as a vision or state but as a recognition: 一切万法、不離自性 — everything that arises, arises from this nature that is already here. Third, the verb 大悟 (great awakening) uses 悟 rather than 覚 or 証, marking this as the sudden-school vocabulary: an event, a break, not a gradual brightening. The passage is the most cited awakening narrative in the Chan/Zen tradition and anchors Huineng's authority as the Sixth Patriarch.

passage: 老人云：某甲非人也。往昔迦葉佛時、曾住此山。因学人問、大修行人還落因果也無。某甲對云：不落因果。五百生墮野狐身。今請和尚代一轉語。丈云：不昧因果。老人於言下大悟。
notes: An old man who has been haunting Baizhang's assembly reveals that he was once a teacher on this mountain, asked by a student whether a great practitioner still falls into cause and effect (因果 — the framework within which 業/karma operates). He answered 不落因果 (not falling into cause and effect) and was thereby reborn as a wild fox for five hundred lives. He asks Baizhang for the correct response; Baizhang gives it: 不昧因果 (not being blind to cause and effect). The old man awakens (大悟) at once. The koan turns on a single character: 落 (fall into, be subject to) versus 昧 (be blind to, deny). The wrong answer denies karma — claiming that an enlightened person is simply free from 業 — and produces five hundred lifetimes of fox-existence as its karmic result. The right answer does not deny karma but refuses ignorance of it: awakening does not suspend 業 but sees it clearly, without the obscuration that generates compulsive reaction. The koan thus models the correct Chan relationship to 業: neither mechanical determinism (you cannot escape cause and effect) nor antinomian denial (enlightenment means freedom from karma), but a clear-eyed, un-blind dwelling in the reality of karmic causation.

passage: 科学万能の夢は破れた
notes: The dream of omnipotent science is shattered. Liang Qichao writing after visiting post-WWI Europe, in which Western science had produced industrial mass killing. The disillusionment with 科学 as a totalizing solution marks a key moment in East Asian intellectual history — the imported concept turning on itself. The sentence is written in Japanese-inflected Chinese, showing Liang's complete fluency in the wasei-kango vocabulary even as he uses it to critique the civilization that produced it.

passage: 不識本心、学法無益。若識本心、見自本性、即名丈夫、天人師、仏。
notes: The Fifth Patriarch Hongren addresses the full assembly: not recognizing your original mind, learning the Dharma is useless; if you recognize the original mind and see your own original nature, you are called a great person, teacher of gods and humans, a Buddha. The parallelism is doctrinal: 識本心 (recognizing the original mind) and 見自本性 (seeing one's own original nature) are presented not as a sequence but as a single recognition — to know the mind is to see the nature. The term 見性 crystallizes this formula: seeing (見) one's nature (性) is not preparation for realization but the recognition itself. The consequence in Hongren's statement — being called 丈夫 (great person), 天人師 (teacher of gods and humans), 仏 (Buddha) — does not indicate a promotion in rank but the revealing of what the recognition always already entails. The passage establishes the Platform Sutra's foundational claim: the nature to be seen is already fully present, and the Dharma's entire function is to occasion its recognition.

passage: 菩提本無樹、明鏡亦非台、本来無一物、何処惹塵埃。
notes: Huineng's verse, presented in the Platform Sutra as the counter to Shenxiu's verse. Shenxiu had compared the mind to a bright mirror requiring constant polishing; Huineng's response systematically negates each image. 菩提 opens the verse: bodhi originally has no tree — not the tree under which Śākyamuni attained awakening, not any locatable object or growable thing. The verse then negates the bright mirror (no stand), then the entire framework of a mind that accumulates dust: originally not a single thing — where could dust arise? The negation of 菩提-as-tree is the negation of awakening as a spatial and temporal achievement. If 菩提 is located nowhere, it cannot be gained through journey or lost through inattention. Huineng's verse has been interpreted as a statement of 本来無一物 (originally nothing whatsoever) — a teaching that can tip into nihilism if misread. But the intended target is not emptiness as void but the framework of seeking-and-attaining that Shenxiu's mirror metaphor encodes. The verse does not say there is no awakening but that awakening does not work the way the mirror metaphor implies.

passage: 不思善、不思惡、正与麼時、那箇是明上座本来面目。惠明言下大悟。
notes: Gateless Gate Case 23. Huineng, having fled with the robe and bowl of Dharma transmission, is caught by the pursuing monk Huiming (Ming the Superior). Instead of fighting, Huineng places the robe and bowl on a rock and says: they are symbols — take them if you can. When Huiming cannot move them, Huineng speaks: without thinking of good, without thinking of evil, at just this very moment — what is your original face (本来面目)? 言下大悟: Huiming attains great awakening at the words. The case demonstrates what 大悟 requires: not a clever answer but the cessation of the discriminating mind's movement between poles (good/evil, self/other, having/not-having). The question about original face cannot be answered by conceptual mind — the very attempt to formulate an answer shows that one has not understood the question. 大悟 happens in the moment the question cuts through rather than when an answer is found. 言下 (at the words, in the instant of hearing) specifies that the great awakening does not follow from processing the question but arrives at the collapse of the framework that made processing seem possible. The passage is the canonical illustration of why Chan insists 大悟 is not achieved but recognized.

passage: 此法門是頓教、亦名大乗、亦名最上乗。迷来経累劫、悟則刹那間。
notes: Huineng characterizes his Dharma gate near the close of the Platform Sutra: it is the sudden teaching (頓教), also called the great vehicle (大乗), also called the supreme vehicle (最上乗). The verse that follows — coming through delusion over countless kalpas, in awakening it is an instant (刹那間) — states the paradox of 頓悟 with maximum compression. The countless kalpas (cumulative aeons of wandering in delusion) are not shortened by efficient practice into a shorter accumulation; they are superseded in a single instant that is structurally outside the sequence they constitute. 刹那 (a Sanskrit term for the briefest unit of time, a moment) is the antithesis of 累劫 (accumulated kalpas) — not a small amount of the same thing but a different kind of time altogether. This is what 頓悟 ultimately claims: awakening does not occur within the timeline of practice but opens into a different temporality. The Platform Sutra's characterization of this teaching as 最上乗 (supreme vehicle) frames the sudden-gradual distinction not as a pedagogical preference but as a claim about the depth of what is being transmitted. The Southern school positions 頓悟 as the teaching suited for the highest capacity — those who can recognize their nature directly, without requiring the scaffolding of graduated practice.

passage: 仏法には身心一如にして、性相不二と談ず。
notes: In the Buddha's teaching it is said that body and mind are one, and that nature and form are not two. The line is a stock formulation associated with Dōgen's writings, paralleling 身心一如 with 性相不二 (nature and appearance not two) — the same non-dualistic claim made twice over, once about the relationship between body and mind and once about the relationship between underlying nature and outward form. The structural rhyme is doing precise work: the doctrine being asserted is not a claim about two specific pairs but a refusal of the dualistic framing that would generate any such pair as a question in the first place. Reading the line carefully, the formula 身心一如 is not arguing for unity against an established dualism but pointing at the actuality that practice engages — what zazen is the activity of, what awakening recognizes — and noting that this actuality cannot be parsed into the dualistic categories that ordinary thought takes for granted.

passage: 何期自性、本自清浄。何期自性、本不生滅。何期自性、本自具足。何期自性、本無動揺。何期自性、能生万法。
notes: How could I have known that the self-nature is originally and of itself pure! How could I have known that the self-nature is originally without arising or perishing! How could I have known that the self-nature is originally fully complete! How could I have known that the self-nature is originally unmoving! How could I have known that the self-nature can give rise to all dharmas! Huineng's exclamation upon awakening at the words of the Diamond Sutra. The five-fold parallel structure is doctrinally compact. Each clause attributes a property to 自性 — purity, freedom from arising and perishing, fullness, immobility, generative capacity — that ordinary thought would treat as separate metaphysical claims. Read together, they describe the awakened recognition of what one's own nature is: not a thing among things but the very basis from which all dharmas arise. The fifth clause is the most striking: 自性 is not merely the nature one sees but is itself capable of generating all phenomena. This is the strongly affirmative use of 自性 in the Chan/Zen tradition — the same term that Madhyamaka denies any phenomenon possesses is here affirmed as the source of all phenomena. The two usages are not contradictory but operate at different levels: what Madhyamaka denies is inherent independent essence; what Chan affirms is the unfindable, generative ground that is itself empty in the Madhyamaka sense. Reading this passage requires holding both registers at once.

passage: 心量広大、猶如虚空、無有辺畔。亦無方円大小、亦非青黄赤白、亦無上下長短、亦無瞋無喜、無是無非、無善無悪、無有頭尾。諸仏刹土、尽同虚空。世人妙性本空、無有一法可得。自性真空、亦復如是。
notes: The capacity of mind is vast, just like empty space, without bound or edge. Without shape or measure, neither blue nor yellow nor red nor white, without high or low or long or short, without anger or joy, without right or wrong, without good or evil, without head or tail. All Buddha-fields are entirely the same as empty space. The wondrous nature of worldly people is originally empty, without a single dharma to be obtained. The true emptiness of self-nature is likewise. Huineng's exposition gives the most extended Platform Sutra account of how mind, self-nature, and the nature of all phenomena (法 / dharmas) are to be understood. The cascade of negations is doing precise work: it strips away every characterization that ordinary thought would attach to either mind or phenomena, until what remains is the bare openness — 虚空 (empty space) — that the doctrine identifies as the nature shared by mind and Buddha-fields alike. The line 無有一法可得 (without a single dharma to be obtained) is the doctrinal core: 法性 here is the absence of any obtainable dharma, the bare openness that remains when grasping ceases. This is the Madhyamaka strain of the Chan/Zen reading of 法性 — the nature of dharmas is precisely their lack of inherent self-existence, and that lack is what mind and Buddha-fields share.

passage: 真如自性起念、六根雖有見聞覚知、不染万境、而真性常自在。
notes: From the true thusness of one's own nature, thoughts arise; the six sense organs see, hear, perceive, and know, yet without becoming stained by the ten thousand objects — and so the true nature always remains free in itself. The passage is one of the Platform Sutra's most direct descriptions of how 真如 operates in the actual functioning of an awakened practitioner. The crucial move is that 真如 is not a static absolute removed from ordinary perception but the very ground from which thoughts arise. The senses continue to work, perception continues to occur — what is missing is the staining (染), the entanglement of awareness with its objects. This is the Chan/Zen reading of 真如 at its most characteristic: not a metaphysical thing to be contemplated but the actual mode in which an unobstructed mind functions. The term 真性 in the same passage glosses 真如 in slightly different language — true nature, true thusness — and the parallelism makes clear that the absolute being pointed at is not separate from the practitioner's own functioning but is precisely that functioning when it is no longer entangled.

passage: 於諸境上心不染、曰無念。於自念上常離諸境、不於境上生心。
notes: On all objects of perception, the mind does not become stained — this is called no-thought. In one's own thoughts, always to be apart from objects, not to give rise to mind upon objects. Huineng's exposition of 無念 in the Platform Sutra, which provides the doctrinal scaffolding for the closely related 無心. The crucial phrase is 不於境上生心 — not giving rise to mind upon objects. This is exactly what 無心 names: not the absence of mind but mind that does not arise as a fixating, grasping movement toward objects. Perception continues; objects appear; the mind remains responsive — but it does not 'give rise to mind upon objects,' does not construct from each appearance the discriminating activity of grasping. Reading this passage clarifies that 無心 is not a doctrine of cessation or emptiness but a doctrine about the mode in which awareness functions when it is not entangled with what it perceives. The freedom 無心 names is the freedom from staining (染) — the discoloring of awareness by attachment to its contents.

passage: 人有南北、仏性本無南北。
notes: People have north and south; Buddha-nature originally has no north or south. Huineng's reply to Hongren when the Fifth Patriarch first questions him at the monastery — Huineng is from the southern barbarian regions and Hongren tests him with the implicit suggestion that southerners are unsuited to attain Buddhahood. The reply collapses the geographic-and-ethnic distinction by appealing to the universality of 仏性: the differences that organize ordinary social and bodily reality (north/south, civilized/barbarian, monk/layperson) do not partition the Buddha-nature, which is the same in all beings without distinction. The line is the canonical Chan/Zen statement of universal Buddha-nature in narrative form, and it is doing political work as well as doctrinal — Huineng's authority as the eventual Sixth Patriarch is legitimized partly by his refusal to be excluded by the geographic prejudice his initial appearance would invite. The doctrine of universal 仏性 underwrites the meritocratic possibility of his eventual succession.

passage: 善知識、我於忍和尚処、一聞言下便悟、頓見真如本性。
notes: Good friends, when I was at the place of Master Hongren, hearing his words a single time I immediately awakened — suddenly seeing my true thusness, my original nature. Huineng's first-person account of his own breakthrough at the Fifth Patriarch's monastery. The temporal markers are doing precise work: 一聞 (hearing once), 言下 (at the words), 便悟 (immediately awakened), 頓見 (suddenly saw). Together they collapse any temporal extension between hearing and awakening — the breakthrough is not the result of extended reflection on what was heard but happens at the moment of hearing itself. This is the structural shape 開悟 takes when it occurs in narrative form: a particular moment, a particular occasion, a particular instant in which what was closed becomes open. Huineng's breakthrough is the canonical instance of 開悟 in the Chan/Zen tradition and serves as the model that subsequent biographical literature consistently echoes.

passage: 若識自性、一悟即至仏地。
notes: If you recognize your own nature, then a single awakening immediately reaches the Buddha-ground. The line is one of the Platform Sutra's strongest formulations of the immediacy of 成仏 in Chan/Zen. Three structural features deserve attention. First, the verb 識 (recognize) places the entire weight of attainment on a cognitive event rather than a cumulative process — what is required is recognition, not production. Second, 一悟 (a single awakening) collapses the temporal extension of the path: there are not many awakenings building toward Buddhahood, only one recognition that arrives complete or has not yet arrived. Third, the verb 至 (reach, arrive at) treats Buddhahood as a place one comes to be at, not a property one acquires — the spatial figure is doing work the temporal figure of 成 alone might miss. Reading 成仏 through this passage, the becoming-Buddha is not the production of a new substance but the arrival at what was always already the case — one's own nature recognized as already on the Buddha-ground.

passage: 大死一番、絶後再蘇。
notes: The great death one time, then revival after the cutting-off. The line is a stock Rinzai formulation associated especially with Hakuin Ekaku but circulating throughout the Chan/Zen instructional literature, including the commentarial tradition surrounding the Gateless Gate. The two halves of the formula must be read together. 大死 alone would be an annihilation; 絶後再蘇 alone would be a recovery without the rupture that makes recovery transformative. The sequence — total cessation followed by re-emergence — is the structural shape of what Rinzai koan practice aims at. The koan drives the discriminating mind to its absolute exhaustion (大死), and what then arises is not the same self that died but a transformed mode of functioning in which the prior structure no longer holds. Hakuin's writings repeatedly insist that without the great death there is no great awakening — that the breakthroughs students sometimes claim without having undergone the cessation are not yet 大悟 but only its anticipations.

passage: 本覚と始覚と一なり、これを修証一等と云ふ。
notes: Original enlightenment and acquired enlightenment are one — this is what is called the oneness of practice and realization. The line is a stock formulation of the Dōgen-derived position that 本覚 and 始覚 cannot be cleanly separated: the practice that arrives at awakening (始覚) is itself the activity of the awakening that was always-already-the-case (本覚). The formula 修証一等 (shushō ittō) makes this collapse explicit and is the cornerstone of Dōgen's theological position. Read carefully, the line is not denying the distinction but refusing the inference that would treat 本覚 and 始覚 as two separable events in time. They are one in the sense that the realization is the realized — there is no temporal gap, no acquisition of something previously absent, only the manifestation of what is.

passage: 一切衆生、本来是仏、生死涅槃、猶如昨夢。
notes: All sentient beings are originally Buddhas; birth-and-death and nirvāṇa are like last night's dream. The line is a stock formulation of hongaku doctrine that circulates throughout East Asian Buddhism and is alluded to in Platform Sutra and later Chan literature. The compression is striking: 本来是仏 — originally already Buddha — flattens the entire soteriological architecture of practice and attainment into a single recognition. The dream simile is doing important work: dreams are real while one is in them and unreal once one wakes, but the waking is not the addition of something new — it is the recognition of what was always the case beneath the dream's seeming. This is the structural claim hongaku makes about the relationship between delusion and awakening, and it is why Dōgen and other Kamakura thinkers had to engage the doctrine carefully — it can be read either as authorizing the most radical practice or as authorizing none.

passage: 法即一種、見有遅疾。何名頓漸。法無頓漸、人有利鈍。故名頓漸。
notes: Huineng addresses the sudden-gradual question directly: the Dharma is of one kind, but seeing has its slow and quick forms. What are 'sudden' and 'gradual' called? The Dharma itself is neither sudden nor gradual; people have sharp and dull faculties — and so they are called sudden and gradual. The passage is the Platform Sutra's most concise statement of the doctrinal frame: 漸悟 names not a different awakening but a different practitioner. The Dharma to be realized is single and indivisible; the variability is in the capacities of those approaching it. Read with the polemic stripped away, this is a remarkably ecumenical position — 漸 and 頓 are not two paths but two paces along the same one. The Platform Sutra elsewhere collapses back into partisan framing, but this passage preserves a more careful theoretical statement of what the distinction was originally about.
